region_of_origin: Asia (widely cultivated across China, Japan, India, and Southeast Asia)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw lotus root is one medium cross-cut segment weighing approximately 100g, often used in stir-fries, soups, or salads.

nutrient_source.title: Lotus root, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for lotus root, raw (cr_id F14040051). All core macros and micronutrients sourced from this entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for raw lotus root is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 53 is estimated based on the closest analogous starchy root vegetable with comparable fibre content (4.9g/100g) and carbohydrate density (17.2g/100g). The high dietary fibre content of lotus root is well-established to attenuate glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 100g serving calculated as (53 × 17.23) / 100 = 9.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (40mg/100g) and exceptionally high potassium (556mg/100g) with a potassium-to-sodium ratio of approximately 14:1, strongly aligned with DASH dietary principles.
hypertension.guidance: Raw lotus root is highly suitable for hypertension management; its outstanding potassium-to-sodium ratio supports vasodilation and blood pressure reduction — prepare by steaming, boiling, or light stir-frying without high-sodium sauces to preserve this benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High dietary fibre (4.9g/100g) with low sugar (0.5g/100g) moderates postprandial glucose response; estimated GI of 53 places lotus root in the low-GI category.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw lotus root is appropriate for type 2 diabetes management due to its low GI (~53), high fibre content that slows glucose absorption, and low sugar content; avoid deep-fried preparations or sweet glazes that would significantly raise the glycaemic and caloric load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.0g/100g), zero trans fat, high dietary fibre (4.9g/100g), and high potassium (556mg/100g) collectively support cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw lotus root is cardioprotective due to its near-zero saturated fat, high soluble fibre that supports LDL reduction, and high potassium that aids arterial compliance; consume steamed or boiled and avoid high-fat or high-sodium cooking methods.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C (44mg/100g) with anti-inflammatory dietary fibre and zero saturated fat supports reduced systemic inflammation relevant to RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw or lightly cooked lotus root is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its high vitamin C content supporting collagen synthesis and immune modulation, and its high fibre supporting gut microbiome diversity linked to reduced inflammatory markers; avoid deep-fried preparations which generate pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lotus root (raw) is the edible rhizome of the aquatic plant Nelumbo nucifera, widely cultivated and consumed across China, Japan, India, Korea, and Southeast Asia. The raw root is characterised by its distinctive cross-sectional appearance with multiple air channels forming a symmetrical hole pattern, a firm and crisp texture, and a mild, slightly sweet flavour. Per 100g, raw lotus root provides 74 kcal, 2.6g protein, 0.1g fat, 17.2g carbohydrate, and an impressive 4.9g dietary fibre. It is exceptionally rich in potassium (556mg/100g) and contains meaningful amounts of vitamin C (44mg/100g), calcium (45mg/100g), and iron (1.16mg/100g), with very low sodium (40mg/100g). Clinically, lotus root is well-suited for individuals managing hypertension (outstanding potassium-to-sodium ratio of ~14:1), type 2 diabetes (low GI ~53, high fibre, low sugar), and cardiovascular disease (negligible saturated fat, high fibre). It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. In Chinese cuisine, lotus root is commonly used in soups (particularly pork rib and lotus root soup), stir-fries with black bean sauce, and pickled preparations. It is also consumed as lotus root chips (deep-fried snack) though this preparation significantly alters its nutritional profile. Raw preparation or light cooking best preserves its vitamin C content and low-GI properties.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14040051) with confidence 0.95. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence medium for GI component only) based on comparable starchy root vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database framework.
notes: saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source — lotus root is a very low-fat plant food with negligible saturated fat). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — lotus root is a plant food and contains no dietary cholesterol). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (naturally zero — unprocessed raw plant food). monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this entry. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for raw lotus root as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg of 44.0mg/100g is referenced in the input health_suitability section and is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for raw lotus root (USDA SR Legacy: ~44mg/100g), included as a supported value. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate — no direct GI measurement for raw lotus root was found in the University of Sydney GI Database; the value is estimated from comparable high-fibre starchy root vegetables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (53 × 17.23) / 100 = 9.1 per 100g serving.
